title: Investors' Appetite for Money-Like Assets: The Money Market Fund Industry after the 2014 Regulatory Reform

source.name: Federal Reserve Bank of New York

summary: This Federal Reserve Bank of New York Staff Report (Number 816, revised June 2018) by Marco Cipriani and Gabriele La Spada uses a quasi-natural experiment to estimate the premium investors place on money-like assets following the 2014 SEC reform of money market funds. The reform increased information sensitivity only for prime MMFs while leaving government MMFs unaffected, triggering a reallocation of over $1 trillion from prime to government funds. The study distinguishes between a desire to preserve money-likeness and a simple flight to safety.
key_points: The 2014 SEC reform reduced money-likeness only for prime MMFs by increasing the information sensitivity of their shares, leaving government MMFs unaffected
Total outflows from prime to government MMFs exceeded $1 trillion following the reform
The estimated premium for money-likeness is 20 basis points for retail investors and 28 basis points for institutional investors
Institutional investors were more affected by the regulation than retail investors
The elasticity of substitution between prime and government institutional MMF shares decreased from 0.50 to 0.11 due to the regulation
Authors distinguish between a desire to preserve money-likeness versus a simple flight to safety
Study uses a difference-in-differences design exploiting differential treatment of prime vs. government MMFs and institutional vs. retail share classes
why_it_matters: This research quantifies the structural shift in the money market fund industry caused by the 2014 SEC reforms, which reshaped where short-term capital flows. Understanding the premium investors place on money-likeness (20-28 bps depending on investor type) helps regulators assess the cost of future reforms. The $1 trillion reallocation from prime to government MMFs also has implications for funding markets and the distribution of credit risk.
market_structure_context: This research directly addresses market structure: it examines how money market fund regulation reshapes investor behavior and capital flows within the short-term funding ecosystem. The 2014 reform altered the relative attractiveness of prime vs. government MMFs, affecting where institutional cash is parked and the broader structure of short-term credit markets.
important_numbers: 20 basis points (premium for money-likeness for retail investors)
28 basis points (premium for money-likeness for institutional investors)
$1 trillion (total outflows from prime to government MMFs)

issues_found: Source is a historical record (published 2017, revised 2018) - more than 90 days before TODAY; confidence slightly lowered
Available only in PDF - only crawl seed provided, not full text
Limited extract in source body - may not capture all key findings or data points from the full paper

human_review.notes: Historical research paper examining money market fund structure post-2014 reform. Relevant to market structure but dated. PDF-only availability limits extraction completeness. Source confidence is moderate due to age and limited extract provided.

notes: Source is a Federal Reserve Bank of New York Staff Report (SR 816). The crawled content represents only a brief abstract and metadata, not the full research paper. Key quantitative findings (20 bps retail premium, 28 bps institutional premium, $1T outflows, elasticity shift from 0.50 to 0.11) are extracted from the abstract. The paper is academic research and does not contain forward-looking market predictions. Historical record dated 2017/2018 - lower relevance for current market structure considerations unless researching regulatory history.
